did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) combined with Binary Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO) for melanoma skin cancer detection. It employs deep learning methods (CNNs, GoogleNet, ResNet-50) for feature extraction and classification of dermoscopic images from ISIC datasets, comparing performance against SVM, Decision Tree, and K-NN.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel CNN-based method combined with Binary Harris Hawk Optimization for melanoma detection. It details specific methodologies, utilizes public ISIC datasets for training and testing, and reports original performance results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) comparing the proposed approach against SVM, Decision Tree, and K-NN, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Melanoma skin cancer detection based on deep learning methods and binary Harris Hawk optimization
brief_summary: This study proposes a robust Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) method combined with Binary Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO) for detecting melanoma skin cancer in dermoscopic images. The researchers utilized public datasets from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC 2016, 2017, and 2019) to train and test the model for classifying images as healthy or non-healthy. The proposed approach employs HHO to optimize segmentation thresholds and feature extraction using pre-trained networks like GoogleNet and ResNet-50. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy metrics, and the results were compared against Support Vector Machine (SVM), Decision Tree, and K-Nearest Neighbors (K-NN) classifiers. The proposed DL-HHO method demonstrated superior performance, achieving accuracy rates of 97.01%, 96.35%, and 98.44% on the ISIC 2019, ISBI 2016, and ISBI 2017 datasets, respectively. The study concludes that integrating deep learning with meta-heuristic optimization significantly enhances melanoma detection accuracy compared to traditional classification techniques.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target for detection and classification. The study classifies images as 'healthy' or 'non-healthy' (where non-healthy includes melanoma). The ISIC 2019 dataset subset used contained 1000 melanoma and benign images. The dataset description mentions 30 melanomas in a random selection.

Used as the negative class ('healthy' or 'benign') in the binary classification task. The dataset description notes the inclusion of '35 melanocytic moles (common)' and '25 dysplastic moles' in a random selection, and the ISIC 2019 dataset contained 'benign images' alongside melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into 'healthy' or 'non-healthy' (melanoma vs. benign) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is 'classifying melanoma images as healthy or non-healthy' using a CNN combined with Binary Harris Hawks Optimization. The study evaluates performance using classification metrics: sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy, on ISIC 2016, 2017, and 2019 datasets. It compares the proposed DL-HHO method against SVM, Decision Tree, and K-NN classifiers.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2016
datasets_used.description: Public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration, sourced from a hospital in Portugal, containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions captured under standardized conditions. The study utilized a subset of this data for classification.

use.training: Used for training the CNN model combined with Binary Harris Hawks Optimization.
use.testing: Used for testing the model; reported accuracy of 96.35%.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: Public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration, sourced from a hospital in Portugal, containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The study utilized a subset of this data for classification.

use.training: Used for training the CNN model combined with Binary Harris Hawks Optimization.
use.testing: Used for testing the model; reported accuracy of 98.44%.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: Public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC Skin Lesions Analysis for Melanoma Diagnosis), derived from the 2019 challenge. It consists of 1000 melanoma and benign images in 24-bit RGB format with a resolution of 768 x 560 pixels. A random selection included 35 melanocytic moles (common), 25 dysplastic moles, and 30 melanomas.

use.training: Used for training the CNN model combined with Binary Harris Hawks Optimization.
use.testing: Used for testing the model; reported accuracy of 97.01%.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2019.notes: The proposed method achieved superior performance compared to Al-masni et al., Barata et al., and Xie et al.

ISBI 2016.notes: The proposed method produced the best-segmented results from the ISBI 2016 pictures. Oliveira et al. achieved results closest to the best-segmented results in terms of Sensitivity.

ISBI 2017.notes: The proposed method achieved the best-segmented result from the ISBI 2017 images. The closest results for Sensitivity and Accuracy were observed in Guo et al. For Specificity, the results of Li & Shen were the closest.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) combined with Binary Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO)
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained networks GoogleNet and ResNet-50 were used for feature extraction

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Used GoogleNet and ResNet-50 for feature extraction
architecture_details.optimization: Binary Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO) used to optimize segmentation thresholds and select optimal features

training_details.preprocessing: Conversion of RGB images to grayscale, morphological operators for post-processing

classification_or_output_head.details: Classifiers SVM, Decision Tree, and K-NN were utilized for the final classification of extracted features

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of RGB images to grayscale
Application of morphological operators for post-processing
Use of Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO) to optimize segmentation thresholds
conclusions: The proposed method, which combines a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with Binary Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO), demonstrates superior performance in detecting melanoma skin cancer compared to traditional classification techniques such as Support Vector Machine (SVM), Decision Tree, and K-Nearest Neighbors (K-NN).

The integration of deep learning methods with the Binary HHO algorithm effectively addresses challenges in image segmentation, such as blurred noise, by optimizing segmentation thresholds and enhancing boundary detection accuracy.

The proposed DL-HHO method achieved high accuracy rates of 97.01% on the ISIC 2019 dataset, 96.35% on the ISBI 2016 dataset, and 98.44% on the ISBI 2017 dataset, outperforming several existing methods in the literature.
Utilizing pre-trained networks like GoogleNet and ResNet-50 for feature extraction contributes to the model's high performance, surpassing other pre-trained methods such as AlexNet and SqueezeNet.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the reliance on public ISIC datasets, which may constrain data diversity and representativeness, and notes that smoothing images can inadvertently damage edges.

Future work requires validation on broader and more diverse datasets, including images with various skin types, lesion sizes, and imaging conditions, to ensure the method's reliability and generalization across different demographic backgrounds and geographic regions.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on public ISIC datasets (2016, 2017, 2019), which may constrain data diversity and representativeness, limiting generalizability to broader populations.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics without significance testing.

The binary classification task ('healthy' vs. 'non-healthy') lacks granularity regarding specific benign lesion subtypes, potentially masking performance variations across different benign conditions.
The paper acknowledges that image smoothing techniques used for segmentation may inadvertently damage lesion edges, presenting a significant challenge for boundary detection accuracy.
The number of images used in experiments was noted as limited, indicating a need for larger, more diverse datasets to enhance the efficacy of the HHO-based deep learning architecture.

External validation on diverse skin types, lesion sizes, imaging conditions, and geographic regions is required to assess real-world applicability and robustness across different demographic backgrounds.
Ground truth validation relies on public dataset annotations; the paper suggests incorporating expert annotations and clinical assessments to enhance reliability.
The study does not report data leakage risks or detailed cross-validation protocols, relying instead on standard train/test splits from public challenges.
